identity.tagline: Croatia's most remote inhabited island — closed to tourists until 1989, still fiercely itself.

practical.day_trip_note: Vis is 2.5 hours from Split by ferry — too far for a comfortable day trip. Plan at least two nights to justify the journey and actually feel the island.

getting_there.ferry_notes: One main ferry route from Split, run by Jadrolinija. Journey takes approximately 2.5 hours. Significantly fewer sailings than Hvar or Brač — check the schedule carefully, especially for return trips. Book in advance in peak season.

ferry_routes.community_note: The only direct ferry connection. Limited sailings — plan around the timetable.

nearby_islands.name: Biševo
nearby_islands.description: A tiny island with the famous Blue Cave (Modra špilja) — one of Croatia's most visited natural attractions.
nearby_islands.worth_visiting_for: The Blue Cave. Go early morning for the best light inside the cave.

nearby_islands.name: Hvar
nearby_islands.description: Croatia's most famous island, a complete contrast to the quietness of Vis.
nearby_islands.worth_visiting_for: If you want nightlife and a busier scene after the tranquillity of Vis.

hidden_gems.title: Stiniva Cove
hidden_gems.description: Consistently rated one of Croatia's most beautiful beaches — a narrow pebble cove reached through a dramatic rock gap. Crowded at midday in summer, magical at dawn or dusk.

hidden_gems.title: Vis town wine bars
hidden_gems.description: Vis produces some of Croatia's best indigenous wines, particularly Vugava white and Plavac Mali red. The wine bars in Vis town are unpretentious and excellent.

local_warnings: Ferry frequency is low — missing the last return sailing means an unplanned extra night on the island.
The Blue Cave on Biševo requires a boat trip and gets extremely crowded in July and August. Book in advance or go in June or September.
